narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your LSAT score of 35 and undergraduate GPA of 3.0. The admissions model predicted a 47.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejection since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—much lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile—and strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles. While your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, it wasn't enough to offset that weaker Year 3 performance, especially since it's a bit below the average of 3.21. Your LSAT score provided some positive counterbalance too, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass, though it fell short of the dataset average of 36.7. Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often have stronger mid-to-late academic deciles and slightly higher test scores, your trajectory raised more red flags for long-term bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black leader in law or your full-time commitment, but rather the model's focus on patterns from past students where lower Year 3 rankings correlated with first-try failures. Many applicants in similar situations have improved by targeting that specific area. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later academic deciles, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA-building experiences. Strengthening your LSAT could amplify those gains, as it already showed promise. Reapplying after addressing Year 3 performance has helped others in your position turn predictions around—reach out to admissions for feedback and explore bridge programs designed for determined students like you. Your background gives you resilience; use it to rewrite this story.
